mainEntity.name: How much does a Jaguar X-Type 3.0 V6 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned X-Type 3.0 V6 engine typically costs £3,500–£5,500 supply only. Fitting adds £800–£1,300 depending on the specialist. Used engines can be found from £495. Main dealer engine replacement quotes have been reported in the £15,000–£30,000 range, while reconditioning or rebuilding is often in the £8,000–£10,000 range. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your X-Type.

mainEntity.name: Is the Jaguar X-Type 3.0 V6 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The AJ30 is a generally robust engine based on the Ford Duratec V6, but it has well-documented vulnerabilities. Common issues include timing chain tensioner wear (rattling on cold start), coolant leaks from the thermostat housing and coolant reservoir, ignition coil failure, and oil leaks from valve cover gaskets. With proper maintenance, the AJ30 can achieve high mileage.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Jaguar X-Type 3.0 V6?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most common issues are timing chain tensioner wear (rattling on cold start — can lead to catastrophic engine failure if ignored), coolant leaks from the thermostat housing and coolant reservoir (cracking plastic components), ignition coil failure (misfires, rough idle), oil leaks from valve cover gaskets, and transfer case failure on AWD models.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an X-Type 3.0 V6 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept X-Type worth £3,000–£5,000, a £4,000–£5,000 recon is 80–100% of the car's value — borderline but often worth it for a cherished example. Against a tired example with significant body or drivetrain issues, the same recon may exceed our 60% Jaguar threshold — get a scrap valuation first.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Jaguar X-Type 3.0 V6 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is AJ30 (also known as AJ-V6 3.0L), found on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation. It's the 3.0-litre 60-degree V6 fitted to the X-Type X400 from 2001–2009. The engine produces 227–231 bhp.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the timing chain tensioner, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The tensioner and guides must be replaced if worn. A preventative replacement costs £600–£1,500. A snapped chain almost always destroys the engine, requiring a £3,500–£6,500+ replacement. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately. Regular oil changes are the key to preventing timing chain issues.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: X-Type 3.0 V6 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch it early — far cheaper than replacement
Piston-valve contact means little is left to repair
Simple replacement — wear and tear item
Worth it on a well-maintained X-Type

buyingChecks.title: Buying This X-Type 3.0 V6? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with regular oil changes (critical for timing chain longevity)
* No cold-start rattling (timing chain tensioner health)
* No coolant loss or overheating history (cooling system health)
* Recent ignition coil replacement (common wear item)
avoidIf: * Cold-start rattling and no timing chain history — budget for tensioner work
* No service history — the AJ30 is sensitive to oil quality
* Coolant loss or overheating history — cooling system damage may have occurred
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis — may indicate timing, coil or sensor problems
* Excessive oil consumption — may indicate bottom-end wear

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The X-Type 3.0 V6 is Jaguar's entry-level V6 in the X400 generation — a 3.0-litre AJ30 engine producing 227–231 bhp. Based on the Ford Duratec V6, it's a generally robust engine with well-documented vulnerabilities: timing chain tensioner wear, coolant leaks from the thermostat housing and reservoir, ignition coil failure, and oil leaks from valve cover gaskets. The engine itself is capable of high mileage with proper maintenance, but the X-Type's reputation for electrical and drivetrain issues (particularly the AWD transfer case) means the rest of the car should be carefully inspected too. With typical X-Type values ranging from £2,000 to £6,000, an engine replacement is a significant investment — a well-maintained example with documented service history is the safest choice.

cta.label: → Compare X-Type 3.0 V6 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Jaguar X-Type 3.0 V6 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or clattering (timing chain tensioner warning) — see the map above
* Service History — Including regular oil changes (critical for timing chain longevity)
* Coolant Level — Check reservoir when cold; any low or loss may indicate thermostat housing or reservoir cracks
* Coolant Leaks — Inspect around thermostat housing and coolant reservoir for cracks or residue
* Ignition Health — Check for smooth idle; any misfire may indicate coil failure
* Oil Leaks — Check around valve cover gaskets for leaks
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes read; unresolved issues may indicate timing, coil or sensor problems
* Transfer Case — Test AWD operation; transfer case failure is a known issue on X-Types
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
